metadata.title: Penrose (Conformal) Diagrams and Causal Structure of Black Hole Spacetimes
metadata.description: Comprehensive database of Penrose diagram structures, causal boundaries, and conformal transformations for various black hole spacetimes

introduction.whatIsPenroseDiagram: A Penrose diagram (Carter-Penrose diagram or conformal diagram) is a 2D representation of a spacetime where: (1) the entire infinite spacetime is mapped to a finite region, (2) light rays always travel at 45° angles, and (3) the causal structure is preserved.
introduction.conformalTransformation: The metric is multiplied by a conformal factor Ω² that maps infinity to finite boundaries while preserving the light cone structure: ds²_physical = Ω² × ds²_diagram

conventions.causalFuture: Region accessible by a future-directed causal (timelike or null) curve from a point

minkowskiSpacetime.diagramShape: Triangle (or diamond, showing both left and right halves)

boundaries.name: i⁺ (future timelike infinity)
boundaries.description: Where all timelike geodesics end (infinite future of all massive particles)

boundaries.name: i⁻ (past timelike infinity)
boundaries.description: Where all timelike geodesics begin (infinite past)

boundaries.name: i⁰ (spacelike infinity)
boundaries.location: Right vertex (or left, depending on convention)
boundaries.description: Spatial infinity — where all spacelike surfaces end

boundaries.name: ℐ⁺ (future null infinity, 'Scri plus')
boundaries.description: Where outgoing light rays end — the future endpoint of all null geodesics directed outward

boundaries.name: ℐ⁻ (past null infinity, 'Scri minus')
boundaries.description: Where incoming light rays originate — the past endpoint of all null geodesics from infinity

conformalTransformation.tortoise_coordinate: r* = r + 2M ln|r/2M - 1| (for Schwarzschild; for Minkowski, r* = r)
conformalTransformation.null_coordinates: u = t - r* (retarded), v = t + r* (advanced)
conformalTransformation.compactified: U = arctan(u), V = arctan(v) — maps (-∞,∞) to (-π/2, π/2)

schwarzschildBlackHole.description: Non-rotating, uncharged black hole — the simplest case

maximalkruskalExtension.description: The maximally extended Schwarzschild solution contains four regions connected at the 'Einstein-Rosen bridge'

regions.label: Region I
regions.name: Exterior (our universe)
regions.description: The region outside the event horizon accessible to distant observers

regions.label: Region II
regions.name: Black hole interior (future)
regions.description: The interior of the black hole — the future of anything crossing the horizon. Contains the spacelike singularity.
regions.accessible: Only by falling in from Region I or III
regions.singularityNature: Spacelike — it lies in the future of every point inside the horizon

regions.label: Region III
regions.name: Parallel exterior universe
regions.description: A second asymptotically flat region — a 'parallel universe' connected to ours via the Einstein-Rosen bridge
regions.accessible: NOT reachable from Region I — no causal path connects them
regions.note: This region is mathematically valid but likely unphysical (it disappears in realistic gravitational collapse)

regions.label: Region IV
regions.name: White hole (past)
regions.description: The time-reverse of the black hole — a singularity that emits matter but cannot absorb it
regions.singularityNature: Spacelike — it lies in the past of every point in this region
regions.note: Time-reverse of Region II. Likely unphysical.

keyFeatures.nature: Null surface at 45° angle in the diagram
keyFeatures.description: The boundary of no return — once crossed, all future-directed paths lead to the singularity
properties: Future event horizon: boundary of Region I/III into Region II
Past event horizon: boundary of Region I/III from Region IV

keyFeatures.nature: Spacelike — horizontal wavy line at the top of Region II
keyFeatures.description: All timelike curves inside the BH end at this singularity
keyFeatures.note: It is NOT a point in space — it is a moment in time (the future) that is unavoidable inside the horizon

keyFeatures.nature: Spacelike — horizontal wavy line at the bottom
keyFeatures.description: The white hole singularity — time-reverse of the future singularity

keyFeatures.location: Center of the diagram (r = 2M, t = 0 surface)
keyFeatures.description: The spatial 'throat' connecting Region I and Region III — a wormhole that is NOT traversable
keyFeatures.note: It pinches off too quickly for any signal to cross

lightCones.description: How light cones tilt in the Penrose diagram
lightCones.farFromBH: Standard ±45° cones, symmetric about the vertical (time) direction
lightCones.nearHorizon: Light cones tilt toward the singularity — the 'outgoing' direction becomes less outgoing
lightCones.atHorizon: The outgoing edge of the light cone is exactly radial (along the horizon) — you can orbit but not escape
lightCones.insideHorizon: Both edges of the light cone point toward the singularity — there is no direction that avoids it

realisticCollapse.description: In a realistic gravitational collapse (Oppenheimer-Snyder), Regions III and IV do not exist
realisticCollapse.diagramDescription: Only the exterior (Region I) and the black hole interior (Region II) appear. The stellar matter collapses along a timelike worldline that replaces the white hole and parallel universe regions.
realisticCollapse.stellarSurface: The collapsing star's surface starts in the past, crosses the horizon, and hits the singularity
realisticCollapse.interiorIsFilled: Inside the collapsing matter, the metric is NOT Schwarzschild but Friedmann-like (homogeneous collapse)

kerrBlackHole.description: Rotating black hole — much richer causal structure than Schwarzschild

maximalkruskalExtension.description: The maximal extension of the Kerr solution contains an INFINITE chain of asymptotic regions connected through the ring singularity

regionsPerBlock.label: I
regionsPerBlock.name: Exterior
regionsPerBlock.description: Outside both horizons — an asymptotically flat region

regionsPerBlock.label: II
regionsPerBlock.name: Between horizons
regionsPerBlock.description: Between the outer (event) horizon and inner (Cauchy) horizon. Timelike singularity is NOT in the future here.

regionsPerBlock.label: III
regionsPerBlock.name: Inside Cauchy horizon
regionsPerBlock.description: Inside the inner horizon, containing the ring singularity and allowing passage to a new asymptotic region

maximalkruskalExtension.infiniteChain: The diagram extends infinitely in the vertical (time) direction, with alternating blocks of exterior and interior regions

outerHorizon.name: Event horizon (outer horizon)
outerHorizon.description: The point of no return for outside observers

innerHorizon.name: Cauchy horizon (inner horizon)
innerHorizon.description: The inner horizon beyond which predictability breaks down

instability.description: The Cauchy horizon is UNSTABLE: infalling radiation is infinitely blueshifted, creating a 'mass inflation' singularity
instability.implication: The interior of a realistic Kerr BH likely does NOT have the full maximal extension. The Cauchy horizon is replaced by a null (or weak) singularity.

ringSingularity.description: The singularity of a Kerr BH is NOT a point but a ring of radius a (in Boyer-Lindquist coordinates)
ringSingularity.note: In the maximal extension, one can pass through the ring to reach a new asymptotic region with 'negative r'. However, this region contains closed timelike curves (time travel!) and is likely unphysical.

closedTimelikeCurves.where: In the Region III (r < r_-) where gφφ < 0 for small enough r
closedTimelikeCurves.implication: Violation of causality — time travel possible in this region
closedTimelikeCurves.note: This is considered unphysical — quantum gravity effects likely prevent it

ergosphere.location: Between r_+ and r_ergo = M + √(M² - a²cos²θ)
ergosphere.onDiagram: Lies within Region I, outside the event horizon
ergosphere.description: In the ergosphere, all observers are forced to co-rotate. The Penrose process can extract energy here.
ergosphere.stationaryLimit: The outer boundary of the ergosphere is called the stationary limit surface — no observer can remain stationary inside it

keyDifferencesFromSchwarzschild: Two horizons instead of one (event + Cauchy)
Ring singularity instead of point singularity
Singularity is timelike and avoidable, not spacelike and unavoidable
Infinite chain of universes in the maximal extension
Closed timelike curves exist in the inner region

reissnerNordstromBlackHole.description: Charged, non-rotating black hole — similar causal structure to Kerr but spherically symmetric
reissnerNordstromBlackHole.diagramStructure: Similar to Kerr: infinite chain of asymptotic regions connected through a timelike singularity
reissnerNordstromBlackHole.cauchyHorizonInstability: Same mass inflation instability as Kerr

extremalCase.condition: Q = M (or Q² = M² in geometric units)
extremalCase.note: Extremal RN has a Penrose diagram similar to Kerr but without the Cauchy horizon instability issues (debated)

kerr_Newman.description: Rotating AND charged black hole — the most general stationary BH solution (with mass, spin, charge)
kerr_Newman.diagramStructure: Same topology as Kerr: infinite chain of blocks with two horizons, ring singularity, and CTC region
kerr_Newman.note: Astrophysical BHs are expected to be effectively uncharged (charge is quickly neutralized by accretion), so Kerr is the relevant solution

schwarzschildCoordinates.note: The apparent singularity at r=2M is a coordinate artifact

tortoiseCoordinate.property: Radial null geodesics satisfy dt = ±dr* (light cones are at 45°)

ingoing.description: Regular at the future event horizon — describes infall

outgoing.description: Regular at the past event horizon

kruskalSzekeres.singularity: T² - X² = 1 (hyperbola at top and bottom)
properties: Regular everywhere except the physical singularity at r = 0
Covers the entire maximally extended Schwarzschild spacetime

penroseCompactification.description: Further compactify Kruskal coordinates to fit the infinite spacetime into a finite diagram
penroseCompactification.transformation: p = arctan(T + X), q = arctan(T - X)
penroseCompactification.result: The entire spacetime fits in a diamond-shaped region with boundaries at p, q = ±π/2

evaporatingBlackHole.description: Penrose diagram for a BH that forms from collapse and then evaporates via Hawking radiation
structure: Flat spacetime (past) — before the collapsing matter
Collapsing matter creates the event horizon
The event horizon shrinks as the BH evaporates
At the endpoint of evaporation, the singularity meets the event horizon
After evaporation: flat spacetime (future) with the information paradox
evaporatingBlackHole.keyQuestion: Does the Penrose diagram have a 'thunderbolt' singularity at the evaporation endpoint, or does the singularity remain hidden?
evaporatingBlackHole.informationParadoxOnDiagram: The Hawking radiation at ℐ⁺ and the infalling matter at the singularity are in two causally disconnected regions — how can information come out?

nakedSingularity.description: A singularity not hidden behind an event horizon — violates cosmic censorship
nakedSingularity.penroseDiagram: Similar to Minkowski but with a timelike singularity at r = 0 visible to all observers

cosmicCensorship.weakForm: Singularities formed from generic initial data are always hidden behind event horizons (Penrose, 1969)
cosmicCensorship.strongForm: Singularities formed from generic initial data are always spacelike (no Cauchy horizon problems)
cosmicCensorship.status: Not proven; counterexamples exist in fine-tuned situations

superextremalKerr.note: Thought to be unphysical — spinning up a BH past extremal requires more and more energy and is prevented by the third law of BH mechanics

sources: Carter, Physical Review 174, 1559 (1968) — Kerr spacetime global structure
Hawking & Ellis, 'The Large Scale Structure of Space-Time' (1973)
Poisson & Israel, PRL 63, 1663 (1990) — Cauchy horizon instability
Penrose, Rivista del Nuovo Cimento 1, 252 (1969) — Cosmic censorship
Dafermos, Communications in Pure and Applied Mathematics 58, 445 (2005) — Cauchy horizon
